events.title: Salvador Allende Wins Presidential Election
events.detail: Salvador Allende of the Popular Unity coalition wins Chile's presidential election with 36.2% of the vote, defeating rightist Jorge Alessandri (34.9%) and Christian Democrat Radomiro Tomic (27.8%). Because he lacked an outright majority, Chile's Congress had to ratify his victory. Allende became the world's first freely elected Marxist head of state.

events.title: CIA Launches 'Track II' — Plot to Prevent Allende from Taking Power
events.detail: The Nixon administration authorizes the CIA to launch 'Track II', a covert operation to instigate a military coup before Allende's inauguration. CIA Director Richard Helms receives direct orders from Nixon and Kissinger to make the Chilean economy 'scream'. The plot includes attempts to kidnap and assassinate General René Schneider, the constitutionalist army commander.

events.title: General René Schneider Assassinated by CIA-Backed Conspirators
events.detail: Chilean Army Commander-in-Chief General René Schneider is shot by right-wing extremists backed by the CIA in a botched kidnapping attempt. Schneider, a staunch constitutionalist who opposed military intervention against Allende, dies three days later. The attack backfires: it galvanizes congressional support for Allende's confirmation and eliminates the key obstacle to his presidency.

sources.name: Nathaniel Davis, The Last Two Years of Salvador Allende

events.title: Allende Inaugurated as President of Chile
events.detail: Salvador Allende is inaugurated as Chile's 29th president after Congress confirms him by 153 votes to 35. He becomes the first Marxist to be democratically elected to the presidency in Latin America. Allende pledges to pursue a 'Chilean road to socialism' through legal and constitutional means, without expropriating small landowners and respecting civil liberties.

events.title: Congress Unanimously Nationalizes Copper — 'The Salary of Chile'
events.detail: The Chilean Congress unanimously passes the constitutional amendment nationalizing the country's copper industry, expropriating US companies Anaconda, Kennecott, and Cerro. Copper accounts for 75% of Chile's export earnings. Allende calls it Chile's 'second independence'. The US companies demand $1.1 billion in compensation; Allende deducts 'excess profits' and offers nothing to Anaconda.

events.title: Agrarian Reform Accelerated — Large Estates Expropriated
events.detail: Allende accelerates Chile's agrarian reform law inherited from the Frei government. In 1971 alone, 1,300 large estates (fundos) covering 3.5 million hectares are expropriated and redistributed to peasant cooperatives. Over three years, Allende's government expropriates 4,400 estates totaling 9.9 million hectares. Rural workers form Peasant Councils that become a political base for Popular Unity.

sources.name: Brian Loveman, Chile: The Legacy of Hispanic Capitalism

events.title: Fidel Castro's 23-Day Visit to Chile Alarms Washington
events.detail: Cuban President Fidel Castro visits Chile for 23 days, meeting workers, students, and miners across the country. The extended visit alarms the Nixon administration and the Chilean right, who use it to stoke fears of Cuban-style communism. US Ambassador Edward Korry cables Washington warning that the visit has galvanized Chilean leftists. Nixon responds by tightening economic pressures.

sources.name: Seymour Hersh, The Price of Power

events.title: Bosses' Strike Paralyzes Economy — Truckers, Merchants Walk Out
events.detail: A confederation of truckers, shopkeepers, and professionals launches a nationwide strike (the 'Paro de Octubre') attempting to bring down Allende by economic paralysis. The CIA channels $8 million to opposition groups through 1970-1973; strike organizers receive covert US funding. Workers' cordones industriales (industrial belts) organize self-defense and keep critical industries running. The strike ends when Allende appoints military officers to his cabinet.

events.title: Popular Unity Increases Vote in Congressional Elections — Coup Plans Accelerate
events.detail: Despite economic disruption, Popular Unity wins 44.2% in congressional elections — up from 36% in 1970 — crushing the opposition's hopes of gaining a two-thirds majority to impeach Allende constitutionally. The result shocks the Chilean right and US officials, who conclude that Allende cannot be removed through democratic means. Military coup planning accelerates in the months following.

sources.name: Pamela Constable & Arturo Valenzuela, A Nation of Enemies

events.title: 'Tancazo' — Failed Tank Rebellion Foreshadows Coup
events.detail: A regiment of tanks under Colonel Roberto Souper surrounds La Moneda Palace in an attempted coup. Loyal army forces under General Carlos Prats suppress the uprising within hours; Allende appears before crowds and declares 'I have not been defeated.' The failed 'tancazo' reveals deep divisions within the military but also demonstrates that constitutionalist officers can still contain coup plotters — for the time being.

events.title: Constitutionalist General Prats Forced to Resign — Pinochet Promoted
events.detail: Wives of senior army officers publicly humiliate General Carlos Prats, the constitutionalist Army Commander, in a staged demonstration organized by coup plotters. Prats resigns, and Allende — tragically misreading Pinochet as a loyalist — appoints Augusto Pinochet as Commander-in-Chief. Prats later flees to Argentina, where DINA assassinates him and his wife with a car bomb in September 1974.

sources.name: Joan Garcés, Allende y la experiencia chilena
Carlos Prats, Memorias: Testimonio de un Soldado

events.title: Navy Purges Constitutionalist Officers — Torture of Sailors Reported
events.detail: Chilean Navy intelligence arrests and tortures dozens of sailors and petty officers suspected of loyalty to Allende, in what becomes known as the 'Valparaíso naval trials'. Officers who try to warn Allende of the coup are arrested. The purge removes the last serious internal obstacle to the naval faction's participation in the September 11 coup. Reports of torture in navy vessels predate the coup itself.

events.title: Military Coup Begins at Dawn — Navy Seizes Valparaíso
events.detail: At 6:00 AM, the Chilean Navy seizes Valparaíso, Chile's main port. Army, Navy, Air Force, and Carabineros act in coordinated nationwide action. Military units take control of radio stations, cut communications, and arrest Popular Unity leaders across the country. The coup is planned by a military junta of four commanders: Pinochet (Army), Leigh (Air Force), Merino (Navy), Mendoza (Carabineros).

events.title: Air Force Jets Bomb La Moneda Presidential Palace
events.detail: Chilean Air Force Hawker Hunter jets fire rockets at La Moneda Palace at approximately 11:52 AM. Allende and a small loyal guard resist with rifles and machine guns. The bombing creates fires throughout the historic building. Television crews and international journalists capture footage of the iconic pink palace in flames — images that become global symbols of the coup and are broadcast worldwide.

events.title: Salvador Allende Dies in La Moneda — Circumstances Disputed
events.detail: Salvador Allende dies inside La Moneda after the military assault. The junta immediately claims he committed suicide with an AK-47 rifle given to him by Fidel Castro. Supporters and family members allege he was assassinated by soldiers who stormed the palace. A 2011 forensic investigation commissioned by Chile's judiciary confirmed death by suicide. His body is initially buried in a secret grave; he is given a state funeral in 1990.

events.title: National Stadium Converted to Mass Detention Center
events.detail: Chile's National Stadium in Santiago is converted into a mass detention and torture center, holding up to 12,000 prisoners in the weeks following the coup. Prisoners are interrogated, tortured, and in many cases executed in the stadium tunnels and locker rooms. Renowned folk singer and theater director Víctor Jara is detained there; his hands are broken before he is killed and his body dumped on a Santiago street on September 16, 1973.

events.title: Víctor Jara Tortured and Executed in National Stadium
events.detail: Celebrated Chilean folk musician and theater director Víctor Jara is arrested on September 12 at the Technical University of Santiago and taken to the National Stadium. Soldiers break his hands — a symbolic act targeting the guitarist who composed protest songs for Allende's movement. He is tortured repeatedly over five days and shot at least 44 times. His body is found in a Santiago street. His murder becomes an international symbol of the regime's brutality.

events.title: Military Junta Issues Decrees: Congress Dissolved, Political Parties Banned
events.detail: Within 48 hours of the coup, the military junta issues Decree Law No. 1, dissolving Congress. Decree Law No. 78 bans all political parties; Decree Law No. 12 declares a state of siege. Thousands of foreign nationals — political refugees from Brazil, Uruguay, and Bolivia — are also rounded up. The junta creates the National Intelligence Directorate (DINA) by secret decree to centralize political repression.

sources.name: Diario Oficial de Chile (Chilean Official Gazette)

events.title: Caravan of Death — Army Executes Prisoners Across Chile
events.detail: General Sergio Arellano Stark leads a helicopter mission across Chile executing prisoners already in military custody in Cauquenes, La Serena, Copiapó, Antofagasta, and Calama. At least 75 prisoners are summarily executed. The operation's purpose appears to be demonstrating to local commanders that no mercy should be shown to leftists and ensuring that Pinochet's authority supersedes local commanders. Families are not notified and bodies are hidden.

sources.name: Patricia Verdugo, Chile, Pinochet, and the Caravan of Death

events.title: United States Formally Recognizes Pinochet Junta
events.detail: The Nixon administration formally recognizes the Pinochet military junta just 13 days after the coup, becoming one of the first governments to do so. Secretary of State Henry Kissinger tells Pinochet: 'We are sympathetic with what you are trying to do here.' The US immediately extends $24 million in food credits that had been withheld from Allende. Recognition grants the junta international legitimacy as the world is still reporting on mass executions.

sources.name: Seymour Hersh, The Price of Power

events.title: DINA Formally Established — Secret Police Created
events.detail: Decree Law No. 521 formally creates the Directorate of National Intelligence (DINA), a secret police force answerable directly to Pinochet. Led by Colonel Manuel Contreras, DINA operates secret detention and torture centers across Chile. DINA agents infiltrate opposition organizations, universities, and labor unions. At its peak, DINA employs 4,000+ agents and an extensive network of informants. It becomes the primary instrument of state terrorism under Pinochet.

events.title: Villa Grimaldi Becomes DINA's Main Torture Center
events.detail: Villa Grimaldi, a former estate in Peñalolén, Santiago, is taken over by DINA and becomes its principal detention and torture center. Over 4,500 people are detained at Villa Grimaldi between 1974 and 1978; at least 226 are killed or disappeared. Torture methods include electric shocks, near-drowning (submarino), and severe beatings. The site is later demolished by DINA in 1978 to destroy evidence; it is now a memorial park.

sources.name: Corporación Parque por la Paz Villa Grimaldi

events.title: Vicaría de la Solidaridad Founded — Catholic Church Defends Victims
events.detail: Cardinal Raúl Silva Henríquez of the Archdiocese of Santiago creates the Vicaría de la Solidaridad to provide legal defense, documentation, and humanitarian assistance to victims of political repression. The Vicaría (replacing the ecumenical Committee for Peace suppressed by Pinochet in 1975) becomes the primary human rights organization in Chile under the dictatorship, documenting over 48,000 cases. Its archives later become crucial evidence for prosecutions.

sources.name: Vicaría de la Solidaridad archives, Archivo Nacional Chile

events.title: General Prats and Wife Assassinated in Buenos Aires by DINA
events.detail: Former Army Commander-in-Chief General Carlos Prats and his wife Sofía Cuthbert are killed in Buenos Aires by a DINA car bomb. Prats had fled Chile after his forced resignation and was writing memoirs critical of Pinochet. The assassination is part of DINA's cross-border assassination program, which later becomes formalized as Operation Condor. DINA agent Michael Townley is later convicted; Manuel Contreras is also convicted in Chile in 2000.

events.title: DINA Shoots Chilean Christian Democrat Leader in Rome
events.detail: Former Chilean Vice President Bernardo Leighton and his wife Ana Fresno are shot on a Rome street by Stefano Delle Chiaie, an Italian neo-fascist working with DINA. Both survive but are permanently disabled. The attack is part of DINA's international assassination program targeting prominent Chilean exiles. It demonstrates the reach of Pinochet's intelligence services into Western Europe.

events.title: Operation Condor Formalized — Southern Cone Regimes Coordinate Terror
events.detail: Intelligence chiefs from Chile, Argentina, Bolivia, Paraguay, Uruguay, and Brazil meet in Santiago under DINA Director Manuel Contreras to formalize Operation Condor — a coordinated system for tracking, kidnapping, torturing, and killing political dissidents across borders. The operation has three phases: intelligence sharing, active cross-border operations, and assassination of targets in Europe and North America. The CIA is briefed on Condor's existence from the beginning.

events.title: 'Shock Treatment' — Chicago Boys Implement Radical Free-Market Reforms
events.detail: Finance Minister Jorge Cauas and a team of economists trained at the University of Chicago under Milton Friedman ('Chicago Boys') implement a radical neoliberal program: tariffs slashed, state enterprises privatized, social spending cut 15-25%, and the peso devalued. The 'shock treatment' causes GDP to fall 13% in 1975 and unemployment to reach 16%, but inflation drops from 340% to 84% by 1977. The reforms become a controversial global experiment in market-oriented economics.

sources.name: CEPAL (UN Economic Commission for Latin America)

events.title: Orlando Letelier Killed by DINA Car Bomb in Washington DC
events.detail: Orlando Letelier, Allende's Foreign Minister and leading exile opposition figure, is killed by a DINA car bomb on Sheridan Circle in Washington DC. His American colleague Ronni Moffitt also dies; her husband Michael Moffitt survives. It is the only state-sponsored assassination on American soil in modern history. DINA agent Michael Townley plants the bomb. Henry Kissinger had received advance warning of the Condor assassination program but did not warn targets.

sources.name: Saul Landau, The Assassination of Orlando Letelier

events.title: Senior Allende Officials Sent to Dawson Island Concentration Camp
events.detail: Approximately 100 senior officials from the Allende government — including cabinet ministers, senators, and party leaders — are sent to a military prison camp on Dawson Island in the Strait of Magellan, one of the most inhospitable locations in the world. Prisoners are subjected to forced labor, cold, and harsh treatment. The internment lasts from 1973 to 1974. The camp's existence becomes known internationally through prisoner testimonies and Amnesty International reports.

events.title: Lonquén Mass Grave Discovered — First Evidence of Disappearances
events.detail: A priest from the Vicaría de la Solidaridad discovers the first confirmed mass grave of the Pinochet regime at Lonquén, an abandoned lime kiln south of Santiago. The remains of 15 peasants from Isla de Maipo — arrested by Carabineros in October 1973 and listed as missing — are identified. The discovery provides the first concrete evidence that 'disappeared' prisoners had been executed and secretly buried, forcing the regime to acknowledge the killings for the first time.

events.title: Condor Phase III — Assassination Plots in Europe Uncovered
events.detail: Operation Condor's Phase III targets prominent exiled Latin American political figures in Europe. French intelligence uncovers a Condor plot to assassinate exiled leaders in Paris in 1976. Condor agents operate in France, Italy, Portugal, and Spain. The US State Department's Patricia Derian eventually presses for action, but the full extent of CIA knowledge of Condor assassinations in Europe remains disputed.

events.title: Colonia Dignidad Used as DINA Torture and Execution Site
events.detail: Colonia Dignidad (later renamed Villa Baviera), a secretive German immigrant colony in the Maule region founded by fugitive Nazi Paul Schäfer, is used by DINA as a remote torture and execution site from 1973. At least 100 political prisoners are brought there; many are executed and buried on the grounds. Schäfer's cooperation with DINA includes use of the colony's isolation to keep prisoners hidden from investigators. Schäfer is convicted of child sexual abuse in 2004.

events.title: MIR Armed Resistance — DINA Systematically Destroys Clandestine Network
events.detail: The Movement of the Revolutionary Left (MIR), the most militant faction of the Chilean left, attempts to maintain clandestine resistance after the coup. DINA systematically infiltrates and destroys MIR networks through informants, torture-extracted information, and targeted assassinations. MIR Secretary-General Miguel Enríquez is killed in a police ambush in October 1974. By 1975, the organization is effectively dismantled inside Chile, with surviving leadership in exile.

events.title: Allende's Secret Burial Location Revealed by Regime
events.detail: After years of uncertainty, the Pinochet government reveals that Salvador Allende's body was buried in an unmarked tomb in the Santa Inés cemetery in Viña del Mar under the name 'Salvador Allende G.' His family had been denied knowledge of his burial location since September 1973. His sister Laura Allende was permitted a private visit. His remains would not receive a proper state funeral until September 1990.

events.title: Carter Administration Cuts Military Aid — Human Rights Leverage Applied
events.detail: President Jimmy Carter's administration cuts US military aid to Chile based on the Pinochet regime's human rights record, implementing a policy consistently opposed by Kissinger. The Inter-American Commission on Human Rights visits Chile in 1976 and documents widespread torture. US Ambassador George Landau is instructed to raise human rights concerns directly with Pinochet. The pressure contributes to the formal dissolution of DINA (1977) and slight reduction in visible repression.

sources.name: Inter-American Commission on Human Rights 1977 report

events.title: DINA Dissolved — Replaced by CNI as International Pressure Mounts
events.detail: Under intense US pressure following the Letelier assassination (which threatened US-Chile relations), Pinochet formally dissolves DINA and replaces it with the National Information Center (CNI). The change is largely cosmetic: CNI inherits DINA's personnel, methods, and infrastructure. Manuel Contreras is removed as director but evades criminal prosecution until 1995. Human rights abuses continue under CNI, though the scale is reduced compared to the peak DINA years of 1973-1977.

events.title: DINA Agent Townley Extradited to US — Exposes Condor Network
events.detail: US-born DINA agent Michael Townley is extradited to the United States and confesses to the Letelier assassination, implicating Cuban exile paramilitary group CORU and senior DINA officials. Townley receives a reduced sentence of 10 years in exchange for cooperation and enters the witness protection program. His testimony provides the first comprehensive insider account of DINA's international operations and Condor's structure, becoming crucial for subsequent prosecutions.

sources.name: US Department of Justice court records

events.title: 1980 Constitution Approved in Fraudulent Plebiscite
events.detail: Pinochet's government holds a plebiscite on a new constitution drafted to guarantee the military's political role and entrench neoliberal economic provisions. The vote is held without electoral registers, in conditions that preclude fair campaigning. Official results claim 67% approval. Opposition parties (still officially banned) and international observers dispute the legitimacy. The constitution, with amendments, remains Chile's fundamental law even after democracy is restored in 1990.

events.title: Chilean Exiles Disappeared in Argentina After Buenos Aires Coup
events.detail: After the March 1976 military coup in Argentina, Operation Condor operations intensify. DINA agents operating in Buenos Aires kidnap and disappear over 150 Chilean exiles in coordination with Argentina's SIDE. The Orletti Motors clandestine detention center in Buenos Aires is used by both Chilean and Argentine agents. Victims are transferred across borders without documentation — the so-called 'second Argentina': Chilean prisoners transferred to Chilean secret sites and vice versa.

events.title: Debt Crisis Collapses Chilean Economy — GDP Falls 14%
events.detail: Chile's neoliberal model collapses in 1982: GDP falls 14%, unemployment reaches 30% (and over 50% in poblaciones), and the banking system requires a $4 billion government bailout — effectively re-nationalizing the banks that had been privatized. The crisis discredits the 'Chicago Boys' model and sparks mass protests. Pinochet is forced to pragmatically reverse many free-market policies while retaining the overarching neoliberal framework.

events.title: Copper Workers Call First National Day of Protest — Mass Movement Begins
events.detail: The Confederation of Copper Workers (CTC) calls Chile's first national day of protest since the 1973 coup. Hundreds of thousands participate across the country through work slowdowns, pot-banging (cacerolazos), and marches. The regime responds with tear gas, water cannon, and live ammunition: 5 people are killed on the day of protest. The protest succeeds in breaking the climate of fear and triggers monthly national protest days that continue for two years.

events.title: Rodrigo Rojas DeNegri Burned Alive by Military Patrol
events.detail: Rodrigo Rojas DeNegri (19, a Chilean-American photographer) and Carmen Gloria Quintana (18, a student) are doused with gasoline and set on fire by an army patrol during a national protest day in Santiago. Rojas dies four days later; Quintana survives with severe burns. The killings provoke international outrage, particularly in the US Congress (Rojas held US permanent residency). The Reagan administration, despite initially defending Chile, eventually condemns the killings.

events.title: Pinochet Declares State of Siege — Press Censored, Curfew Imposed
events.detail: As protests continue, Pinochet declares a full state of siege in November 1984, the most repressive measure since 1973. Independent media are censored, political meetings prohibited, and night curfews imposed in major cities. The state of siege lasts until June 1985. During this period, the 'Degollados' case occurs: three Communist Party leaders (Manuel Guerrero, José Manuel Parada, Santiago Nattino) are abducted and murdered by CNI agents, their throats cut.

events.title: FPMR Ambushes Pinochet's Motorcade — He Narrowly Escapes
events.detail: The Manuel Rodríguez Patriotic Front (FPMR), the armed wing of the Communist Party, ambushes Pinochet's motorcade in the Maipo Canyon with RPG rockets and automatic weapons. Five bodyguards are killed; Pinochet escapes with a minor hand wound. The assassination attempt backfires: Pinochet declares a state of siege, and the failed attack allows him to portray opposition as Communist terrorists. The international community rallies around the plebiscite path rather than armed resistance.

events.title: 'The Slashed Throats' — Three Communist Leaders Killed by CNI
events.detail: Three leftist leaders — sociologist José Manuel Parada, graphic designer Manuel Guerrero, and journalist Santiago Nattino — are abducted by CNI agents and found with their throats cut in Santiago. The murders, known as the 'Degollados' (slashed throats) case, shock Chilean society and the Catholic Church. In 1994, a civilian court sentences several CNI officers to life imprisonment — one of the first successful prosecutions of regime agents while Pinochet remained active.

events.title: Former President Eduardo Frei Montalva Dies — Family Suspects Regime Involvement
events.detail: Former Chilean President Eduardo Frei Montalva — who had initially welcomed the 1973 coup but later turned against Pinochet — dies following routine hernia surgery at a Santiago clinic. His family believes he was deliberately infected with a lethal agent by regime agents. A 2009 Chilean court found forensic evidence consistent with a lethal bacterial infection deliberately introduced during his surgery. DINA is suspected; the case remains officially unresolved.

events.title: Reagan Administration Reverses Carter Policy — 'Quiet Diplomacy' Restores Ties
events.detail: The Reagan administration ends Carter's human rights pressure on Pinochet, restoring military aid and credits, voting in favor of Chile in international financial institutions, and pursuing 'quiet diplomacy' instead of public condemnation. UN Ambassador Jeane Kirkpatrick visits Chile and meets Pinochet. The shift is justified by anti-Communist Cold War logic. Human rights groups argue Reagan's embrace gave the regime renewed international legitimacy during a period of continued repression.

events.title: National Accord — 11 Opposition Parties Unite Against Pinochet
events.detail: Eleven political parties ranging from Christian Democrats to Socialists sign the National Accord for Transition to Full Democracy, mediated by Cardinal Francisco Fresno. The document calls for elections, an end to political persecution, and restoration of civil liberties. While Pinochet rejects the accord, it marks the first formal unification of the democratic opposition and establishes the framework for the 1988 plebiscite campaign.

era: The Plebiscite and Transition to Democracy (1988–1990)

events.title: 'No' Campaign Launched — Creative Opposition Strategy Defies Fear
events.detail: The democratic opposition launches the 'No' campaign for the October 5, 1988 plebiscite on whether Pinochet should continue as president for another 8 years. The campaign's creative director Eugenio García uses a rainbow logo and upbeat jingle to break with the imagery of mourning and fear. Access to 15 minutes of daily television time (required by the 1980 constitution) allows the campaign to reach millions of Chileans. The 'No' campaign becomes a model for peaceful democratic resistance worldwide.

events.title: Plebiscite: 'No' Wins 55.99% — Pinochet's Rule to End
events.detail: The plebiscite on whether Pinochet should continue in power results in 55.99% voting 'No' and 44.01% voting 'Yes', on a 97% voter turnout. Pinochet attempts to ignore the result and declare a state of emergency, but his three fellow junta members refuse to support him. In the early hours of October 6, Pinochet concedes. The result triggers mandatory presidential elections for December 1989. The plebiscite is considered one of the most significant democratic victories in Latin American history.

events.title: Patricio Aylwin Wins Presidential Election — Democracy Restored
events.detail: Patricio Aylwin of the Concertación coalition wins Chile's first democratic presidential election since 1970, capturing 55.2% of the vote. Aylwin had been a key anti-Allende politician in 1973 who later became an opponent of Pinochet. His coalition — an alliance of center and left parties — wins a majority in Congress. The election is considered free and fair by international observers. Pinochet retains the position of Army Commander-in-Chief under the 1980 constitution.

events.title: Amnesty Decree Shields Regime Agents from Prosecution
events.detail: Pinochet issues Decree Law No. 2191, granting amnesty to all who committed crimes between September 11, 1973 and March 10, 1978 — the most intense period of DINA repression. The decree is specifically designed to prevent prosecution of military and DINA officers for the thousands of killings and disappearances. Courts initially apply the amnesty broadly, but from the 1990s onwards judges begin finding ways around it, ruling that crimes against humanity are not amnistiable under international law.

events.title: Patricio Aylwin Inaugurated — Democracy Restored After 17 Years
events.detail: Patricio Aylwin is inaugurated as President of Chile on March 11, 1990, in the National Congress building in Valparaíso. The ceremony is attended by heads of state from across the Americas. Pinochet hands over the presidential sash and sits in the front row as Commander-in-Chief of the Army — a reminder that the transition was negotiated, not complete. Aylwin immediately creates the Rettig Commission to investigate human rights violations and promises 'justice to the extent possible'.

events.title: Rettig Commission Established — Truth and Reconciliation Begins
events.detail: President Aylwin creates the National Commission on Truth and Reconciliation, chaired by former senator Raúl Rettig. The commission is given nine months to document cases of death, forced disappearance, and torture resulting in death during the dictatorship. Its 1991 report identifies 2,279 victims of political violence and human rights violations. The report stops short of naming living perpetrators (to avoid obstruction) but provides comprehensive documentation of state terror.

events.title: Rettig Report Released — 2,279 Documented Victims
events.detail: The Rettig Commission publishes its 1,800-page report documenting 2,279 victims of human rights violations under the Pinochet regime, including 2,115 killed or disappeared after arrests. President Aylwin presents the report on national television and asks for the nation's forgiveness, weeping publicly. Pinochet calls the report 'one-sided' and the Army refuses to accept its findings. The report creates the first official government acknowledgment of systematic state terrorism.

events.title: Pinochet Arrested in London on Spanish Warrant — Legal Drama Begins
events.detail: Former dictator Augusto Pinochet is arrested at a London clinic by British police acting on a Spanish arrest warrant from Judge Baltasar Garzón, charging him with torture and conspiracy to murder Spanish citizens in Chile. The 83-year-old Pinochet claims diplomatic immunity and ill health. The case tests whether former heads of state can be prosecuted for crimes against humanity in third countries under universal jurisdiction. After 503 days under house arrest, Britain releases him on medical grounds in March 2000.

events.title: Valech Commission Documents 27,255 Survivors of Torture and Political Imprisonment
events.detail: The National Commission on Political Imprisonment and Torture (Valech Commission), chaired by Bishop Sergio Valech, receives over 35,000 testimonies and recognizes 27,255 survivors of political imprisonment and torture (out of 35,868 who came forward). The report establishes a monthly reparation pension for survivors. The 2011 update raises the total to 40,018 victims. The Valech Commission's work represents the most comprehensive accounting of torture survivors in Chilean history.

events.title: DINA Chief Manuel Contreras Convicted — First Major Pinochet-Era Prosecution
events.detail: Former DINA Director Manuel Contreras and former DINA operations chief Pedro Espinoza are convicted by Chile's Supreme Court for the 1976 assassination of Orlando Letelier. Contreras is sentenced to 7 years; Espinoza to 6 years. When Contreras refuses to turn himself in, a constitutional crisis erupts — Pinochet publicly supports him — before he is eventually imprisoned. The conviction represents the first successful criminal prosecution of a senior Pinochet regime official.

events.title: Pinochet Dies Under House Arrest — 300+ Criminal Cases Pending
events.detail: Augusto Pinochet dies of a heart attack on December 10, 2006 — International Human Rights Day — at age 91, while under house arrest with more than 300 criminal cases pending against him. He is never convicted of any crime. After his London arrest weakened his immunity protections, Chilean courts had stripped him of immunity and issued multiple indictments, but proceedings were repeatedly suspended due to health. His death triggers simultaneous public celebrations and military mourning in Chile.

events.title: Allende Given State Funeral — Buried with Full Presidential Honors
events.detail: On the 20th anniversary of his 1970 election victory, Salvador Allende's remains are exhumed from a secret grave in Viña del Mar and given a full state funeral in Santiago. President Aylwin attends. Allende had been buried in an unmarked grave since September 1973; his family was only permitted to hold a private burial. The state funeral is a symbolic repudiation of the coup and a recognition of Allende's legitimate democratic legacy.
